findings.description: The core typography/font-pairing table (Agenda/Nunito Sans/Aptos per level and per channel, including the Agenda-as-live-text-in-digital-for-campaign-headline-only exception) is entirely unrepresented by any candidate rule. This is a major brand governance table, not a stylistic aside.

payload.description: Font-per-channel mapping (Agenda print headlines, Nunito Sans digital/body, Aptos Office fallback) with digital campaign-headline live-text exception for Agenda.

findings.description: The entire email type scale block (H1–H3 sizes, body 16px minimum, secondary 14px sparingly, footnote 12px, absolute 12px floor) contains multiple distinct hard constraints but none were extracted as rules; only the unrelated line-height rule (u_0019) was captured downstream.

payload.note: Split into at least: type-scale-by-level rule, body-16px-minimum rule, secondary-text-usage rule, footnote-must-be-smaller rule, absolute-12px-floor rule.

findings.description: The [GENERAL]-tagged email CSS font-stack rule (with @font-face/Google Fonts requirement and Outlook fallback rationale) is missing entirely as a rule and, if added, needs an explicit channel=email governance facet to distinguish it from the general typography table.

Rule captures the 150% minimum line-height but drops the [GENERAL]-tagged conditional exception (dark-background text must use 160%, e.g. 16px/26px), which is a distinct governance-scoped sub-requirement.

Source uses absolute prohibitive language ('Never use two adjacent text blocks at the same size+weight...') which indicates hard_constraint, but rule is tagged strong_default, understating enforceability.

Available weight lists per font (Agenda, Nunito Sans, Aptos) act as usage constraints tied directly to the font table but are not clustered into any rule, leaving the font-hierarchy governance incomplete.

payload.description: Only listed weights per font family (Agenda/Nunito Sans/Aptos) may be used.

findings.description: Missed relation: the 16px body-copy minimum size (u_0015) and the 150% line-height minimum (u_0019) are companion accessibility constraints ('bigger is better') that should be linked or co-clustered, but the size-minimum half is missing as its own rule entirely.
